content:
# Romans Chapter 10 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 10 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 10:1 — "heart's desire" vs. "desire of the heart of me" vs. "longing of my heart"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Brothers, the desire of the heart of me and the prayer to the God for them [is] for salvation." | Literal possessive chain; "the God" with article |
| LOGOS | "Brothers, my heart's desire and prayer to God for them is that they may be saved." | Compact English possessive; purpose clause |
| PNEUMA | "Dear brothers and sisters, the longing of my heart and my prayer to God is for the people of Israel to be saved." | Adds "and sisters," "dear," specifies "the people of Israel" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "brothers and sisters" is a common inclusive rendering but ἀδελφοί in context refers to fellow believers generally. PNEUMA's identification of "them" as "the people of Israel" is accurate but makes explicit what Paul leaves implicit.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:2 — "zeal for God, but not according to knowledge" vs. "misdirected zeal"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they have a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge" | Literal; "zeal of God" (genitive) |
| LOGOS | "they have a zeal for God, but not according to knowledge" | "Zeal for" (objective genitive interpretation) |
| PNEUMA | "I know what enthusiasm they have for God, but it is misdirected zeal." | Replaces "not according to knowledge" with "misdirected zeal" — a summary interpretation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "misdirected zeal" collapses the nuance of "not according to knowledge" (οὐ κατ᾽ ἐπίγνωσιν) into a single adjective, losing the distinction between zeal (real) and knowledge (lacking).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:3 — "seeking to establish their own" righteousness

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "seeking to establish their own [righteousness], they did not submit to the righteousness of the God" | Supplies "righteousness" in brackets |
| LOGOS | "seeking to establish their own, they did not submit to God's righteousness" | Leaves "their own" as elliptical |
| PNEUMA | "Refusing to accept God's way, they cling to their own way of getting right with God by trying to keep the law." | Paraphrases extensively; adds "by trying to keep the law" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with the understanding that "their own" refers to "their own righteousness" from context. PNEUMA's "way of getting right with God by trying to keep the law" is interpretively accurate but far exceeds the Greek text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:4 — "end of the law" vs. "accomplished the purpose for which the law was given"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For [the] end of [the] law [is] Christos, for righteousness to everyone believing." | Literal; retains ambiguity of τέλος; "Christos" |
| LOGOS | "For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes." | Same ambiguity preserved; natural English word order |
| PNEUMA | "For Christ has already accomplished the purpose for which the law was given. As a result, all who believe in him are made right with God." | Resolves τέλος exclusively as "purpose/goal"; adds "already," "as a result" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, which preserves the crucial ambiguity of τέλος (both "end/termination" and "goal/purpose"). PNEUMA resolves the ambiguity entirely in favor of "purpose," losing the "termination" sense that is also present. EMET and LOGOS both wisely retain the double meaning through the English word "end."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The ambiguity of τέλος is deliberately preserved.

---

## 10:5 — "the person who does the commandments shall live by them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the human having done these things will live by them" | "Human" for ἄνθρωπος; participial form |
| LOGOS | "the person who does the commandments shall live by them" | "Person" for ἄνθρωπος; "commandments" specified |
| PNEUMA | "the law's way of making a person right with God requires obedience to all of its commands." | Full paraphrase; drops the Leviticus quotation form |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the direct quotation character (from Leviticus 18:5). The passage functions as a scriptural citation and should retain that form.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:8 — "the word of faith that we proclaim" vs. "the very message about faith that we preach"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the word of the faith which we proclaim" | "Word of the faith" — with article |
| LOGOS | "the word of faith that we proclaim" | Without article; standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "And that message is the very message about faith that we preach" | "Message about faith" changes genitive relationship; "very" added for emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "message about faith" changes the genitive from characterizing ("the faith-word") to objective ("the message concerning faith"), subtly altering the meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:9 — Confession order: "Lord Jesus" vs. "Jesus is Lord"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if you confess with the mouth of you [the] Lord Yeshua, and believe in the heart of you that the God raised him from [the] dead, you will be saved." | "Lord Yeshua" — accusative predicate order as in Greek; retains "Yeshua" |
| LOGOS | "if you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved." | "That Jesus is Lord" — explicit predication |
| PNEUMA | "If you openly declare that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved." | "Openly declare" for ὁμολογήσῃς; otherwise similar to LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The explicit predication "that Jesus is Lord" clarifies the confessional content. PNEUMA's "openly declare" adds a public dimension not necessarily in ὁμολογέω (which means "confess" or "agree"). EMET's "Lord Yeshua" preserves Greek syntax but obscures the creedal statement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:10 — "justified" vs. "made right with God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "with [the] heart [one] believes for righteousness, and with [the] mouth [one] confesses for salvation" | "For righteousness" — εἰς δικαιοσύνην |
| LOGOS | "with the heart one believes and is justified, and with the mouth one confesses and is saved" | "Is justified" — interpretive rendering of εἰς δικαιοσύνην |
| PNEUMA | "it is by believing in your heart that you are made right with God, and it is by openly declaring your faith that you are saved" | "Made right with God" — dynamic equivalent; adds "with God" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Is justified" captures the Pauline forensic language. PNEUMA's "made right with God" is accurate but loses the technical theological term that Paul has developed throughout Romans.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:11 — "Everyone who believes" vs. "Anyone who trusts"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Everyone believing upon him will not be put to shame." | Participle; "upon him" |
| LOGOS | "Everyone who believes in him will not be put to shame." | Relative clause |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who trusts in him will never be disgraced." | "Trusts" for πιστεύων; "never be disgraced" for οὐ καταισχυνθήσεται |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "trusts" is a valid synonym but "believes" maintains consistency with the πιστ- word group used throughout Romans. "Disgraced" is adequate but "put to shame" better captures the LXX language of the Isaiah quotation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:14 — "someone preaching" vs. "someone tells them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "How then will they call upon [him] in whom they did not believe? And how will they believe [in him] of whom they did not hear? And how will they hear without [one] proclaiming?" | "Proclaiming" for κηρύσσοντος; supplies pronouns in brackets |
| LOGOS | "How then will they call on him in whom they have not believed? And how are they to believe in him of whom they have never heard? And how are they to hear without someone preaching?" | "Preaching" for κηρύσσοντος |
| PNEUMA | "But how can they call on him to save them unless they believe in him? And how can they believe in him if they have never heard about him? And how can they hear about him unless someone tells them?" | "Tells them" for κηρύσσοντος; adds "to save them," "about him" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "tells them" domesticates the formal proclamation language (κηρύσσω = herald, proclaim) into casual speech. The missionary chain Paul constructs uses deliberate, elevated vocabulary that "tells" does not convey.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:15 — "How beautiful are the feet" — quotation handling

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "How beautiful [are] the feet of the [ones] proclaiming good news of good things." | Literal; "good news of good things" (longer reading) |
| LOGOS | "How beautiful are the feet of those who preach the good news!" | Shorter; "preach the good news" |
| PNEUMA | "How beautiful are the feet of messengers who bring good news!" | "Messengers" added; not in Greek text |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA adds "messengers" which, while interpretively helpful, is not in the Greek text. EMET's "good news of good things" may reflect a longer textual variant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:17 — "word of Christ" — textual variant

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the faith [is] from hearing, and the hearing through [the] word of Christos" | "Word of Christos" — follows Sinaiticus reading ῥήματος Χριστοῦ |
| LOGOS | "faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ" | Same reading, anglicized |
| PNEUMA | "faith comes from hearing, that is, hearing the Good News about Christ" | Interprets "word of Christ" as "Good News about Christ"; changes genitive relationship |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "Good News about Christ" interprets the genitive as objective (the message about Christ), but the Greek ῥήματος Χριστοῦ could equally be subjective (the word spoken by Christ). The ambiguity should be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:18 — "all the earth" / "ends of the world" vs. "ends of the inhabited world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Into all the earth went out the sound of them, and into the ends of the inhabited [world] the words of them." | "Inhabited world" for οἰκουμένη |
| LOGOS | "Their voice has gone out to all the earth, and their words to the ends of the world." | "World" for οἰκουμένη |
| PNEUMA | "The message has gone throughout the earth, and the words to all the world." | "Message" for φθόγγος (sound/voice); restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "inhabited world" is more precise for οἰκουμένη but LOGOS's "world" is standard for Psalm 19:4 quotations. PNEUMA's "message" replaces "voice" (φθόγγος), losing the acoustic metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:19 — "foolish nation" vs. "foolish Gentiles"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "by a nation without understanding I will anger you" | "Nation without understanding" for ἔθνει ἀσυνέτῳ |
| LOGOS | "with a foolish nation I will make you angry" | "Foolish nation" |
| PNEUMA | "I will provoke your anger through the foolish Gentiles" | "Foolish Gentiles" — interprets ἔθνος as specifically Gentiles |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "Gentiles" is an interpretive leap; the Deuteronomy quotation uses "nation" (ἔθνος singular), and while Paul may imply Gentiles, the quotation itself should be preserved as "nation."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:21 — "held out my hands" vs. "stretched out the hands of me" vs. "opened my arms"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The whole day I stretched out the hands of me to a people disobeying and contradicting." | Literal; "disobeying and contradicting" |
| LOGOS | "All day long I have held out my hands to a disobedient and contrary people." | "Held out" — standard; adjectives rather than participles |
| PNEUMA | "All day long I opened my arms to them, but they were disobedient and rebellious." | "Opened my arms" — more intimate image; adds "but"; "rebellious" for ἀντιλέγοντα |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Held out my hands" preserves the gesture language of the Isaiah quotation. PNEUMA's "opened my arms" shifts from a gesture of supplication/invitation to an embrace metaphor, which is slightly different. EMET's "contradicting" for ἀντιλέγοντα is precise but "contrary" captures it well.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA's interpretive additions and paraphrases | vv.3, 4, 5, 8, 14, 15, 17 | Adds explanatory clauses; resolves deliberate ambiguities |
| Theological term consistency (πιστ-/δικ- word groups) | vv.4, 10, 11 | "Believes" vs. "trusts"; "justified" vs. "made right with God" |
| Preserving vs. resolving ambiguity (τέλος) | v.4 | "End" preserves double meaning; "purpose" resolves it |
| Quotation integrity (retaining OT citation form) | vv.5, 15, 18, 19, 21 | PNEUMA often restructures quotations into paraphrases |
| EMET's untranslated terms | vv.4, 9, 17 | "Christos," "Yeshua" |
| Genitive relationship interpretation | vv.8, 17 | "Word of faith/Christ" — subjective vs. objective |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 10 centers on two critical theological terms: τέλος (v.4) and κύριος (v.9), both of which carry layered meanings that resist simple translation. The most consequential disagreement is v.4, where PNEUMA resolves the τέλος ambiguity while EMET and LOGOS preserve it — SOPHIA sided with preservation. Throughout the chapter, PNEUMA consistently restructures Old Testament quotations into modern paraphrases, losing their citation character. LOGOS again produced the most frequently adopted renderings, balancing fidelity to the Greek with English readability. The chapter also reveals EMET's commitment to preserving Hebrew/Greek names (Yeshua, Christos, Esaias) against LOGOS and PNEUMA's anglicization — SOPHIA consistently opted for the anglicized forms in the consensus text.

---
